title: Chapter 15: Handshake on the Ash Track
paragraphs: Three moons had passed since that night meeting in the clearing. The fading world continued to slowly crumble into dust, but for Yashil, something had subtly changed. Each night, when leaden clouds hid the pale stars, Kirah would silently approach the edge of the ravine. The giant demon no longer hid his scars. He brought the goblin rare sweet roots he found in the wastelands, and Yashil told him about how the world outside the legions of the Abyss was structured.
They were a strange pair, hidden from prying eyes by thick darkness. But by day, Yashil remained alone. He still sat on his ledge, looking at the Great Road, paved with white stone.
That morning, the air was particularly heavy, motionless. It smelled of an approaching dust storm and old iron.

An elven caravan crawled slowly along the tract. Three heavy wagons of dark wood, laden with the remains of provisions from the southern outposts. They were accompanied by only four guards. In past eras, the High Elves traveled under the protection of hundreds of shining spears, but now their resources were exhausted. They looked tired; their proud posture bent under the invisible weight of the slow end.
Yashil watched them, lazily twiddling a dry blade of grass in his fingers. And suddenly his keen hearing caught a rustle. It was not the wind.

From the dry thickets on both sides of the road, shadows silently slipped out. A dozen ragged figures: feral half-orcs, deserters from mercenary units, whose faces were hidden by dirty rags. This was not an army of evil—it was a flock of hungry, desperate vultures for whom a piece of bread was worth more than anyone's life.
The attack happened in complete silence. No battle cries. Only the dull thud of crossbow bolts thudding into the wood of the wagons and the wet sound of tearing flesh.
Two elven guards fell in the first few seconds, struck by poisoned arrows. The third managed to bare his blade, but he was crushed by mass, thrown into the gray dust.

Only one remained. A young elf with flame-red hair, on whose face frozen an expression of deep, almost childish misunderstanding. He retreated to the last wagon, covering a wounded old merchant. His sword trembled. Malefactors were surrounding him, baring rotten teeth in anticipation of easy prey.
Yashil, sitting on the edge of the ravine, froze.

In his head echoed old Griz's words: "Become a shadow. Become a rat. Don't go near the high peoples—they'll kill you at the first opportunity." His mind screamed at him to stay in place. He is a goblin. He has no magic. He has no strength. He weighs less than the armor of one of these bandits.
But his heart, forged from the same matter as his mother's last breath, made a choice before his mind could stop it.
Yashil broke into a run. He tumbled down the slope of the ravine, scraping his knees until they bled, grabbed a thick, smoldering branch from someone's abandoned campfire, and burst onto the tract.

A tiny, clay-smeared green figure with a piercing, ear-splitting shriek crashed into the back of a huge half-orc. The smoldering branch struck the marauder right under the knee. The half-orc howled in surprise and turned around, his scar-marred face twisted in rage.
"A goblin?!" the marauder growled, not believing his eyes. The absurdity of the situation for a second paralyzed even the attackers. A goblin protecting an elf? This broke all the laws of nature.
"Get away from him!" Yashil shouted, holding the smoking stick out in front of him. His knees were shaking, but he didn't take a single step back.
However, the blow did not come.
Instead of the sound of rending flesh, there was a heavy, soft sound of something very large landing. It smelled of wet dog, damp wool, and wild forest.
"Augh," came a deep, velvety, and absolutely lazy voice.
Yashil opened his eyes.

Right in front of him, blocking the half-orc, stood a werewolf. He was tall, lean, and incredibly muscular. An elongated wolf muzzle expressed the extreme degree of philosophical boredom. On one of his shoulders lay a giant double-bladed axe, the size of Yashil himself. The werewolf's amber eyes slid indifferently over the band of marauders.

"Do you know what your problem is, gentlemen?" the werewolf drawled, a free clawed paw scratching behind a pointed ear. "You attack one as a crowd. And as they said in my pack... the wolf is weaker than the lion and the tiger, but the wolf does not perform in the circus. I, of course, left the pack because they didn't get my charisma, but you catch the essence."
The half-orc, coming to his senses, charged at the newcomer with a roar.

The werewolf didn't even change his posture. He just lazily moved his shoulder, and the heavy axe, describing a short arc, struck flat against the attacker's chest. The sound of broken ribs echoed along the tract. The half-orc flew five steps and went still.
The rest of the bandits backed away.

"One can be insanely first," the werewolf declared profoundly, looking at the writhing body. Then he turned his amber gaze to the others. "I am Dzhanavar. A lone wolf. And I am very lazy about running through the bushes after you. So how about you just pretend you were never here. Otherwise, I'll start giving out quotes, and mine are deadly. Augh."

There was no rage in his voice, but from one look at his relaxed, confident power, the marauders realized: they would be torn to pieces in a couple of heartbeats. Dropping their weapons, they scattered, dissolving into the gray mist of the wastelands.
Silence again descended on the Great Road.
Dzhanavar yawned, showing an impressive row of snow-white fangs, thudded his axe into the ground with a clang, and turned to Yashil. The wolf's muzzle widened into a broad, fanged grin.

"Nice one, brother," Dzhanavar sat on his haunches to be on the same level as the goblin. "Jumping out at a crowd with a digging stick? That's either a high degree of courage or an absolute zero of self-preservation instinct. In any case—respect. A true predator is defined not by the size of its fangs, but by the size of its spirit."
"I... I just couldn't watch him being killed," Yashil replied quietly, dropping the smoking branch.
The werewolf chuckled and nodded toward the wagon.
"Well, go on. Accept the thanks, savior."
Yashil slowly turned around.

A red-haired young elf, breathing heavily and clutching a bleeding wound on his shoulder, came closer. His name was Taeron. All his life, from earliest childhood in the marble halls, he had been taught that goblins were dirt. Unprincipled, cowardly thieves, deserving only the edge of a blade. And now he stood before a tiny, trembling green organism in rags, who had just rushed to certain death for him.
Cognitive dissonance was literally readable on the elf's pale face. He looked at Yashil as if he were seeing a living myth.
Taeron lowered his sword. The blade touched the white stone of the tract with a ring.

The elf slowly dropped to one knee so as not to look down on Yashil. A painful internal struggle, breaking centuries of prejudice, was reflected on his face. And then something happened that this fading world had not seen since its creation.
Taeron extended his clean, thin, aristocratic hand with long fingers toward the goblin.

Yashil froze. He looked at this palm, and then at his own—rough, green, covered in old callouses and dirt. The little goblin swallowed a lump in his throat. He timidly, as if afraid it was an illusion that would vanish at a touch, reached out his hand in return.
Their fingers closed. Elf and goblin. Light and shadow.
"My name is Taeron," the guard said hoarsely, barely audible. "And I... I am in unpayable debt to you. I swear by the forces of the universe, I will never forget what I saw today."
Dzhanavar, watching this scene, sniffled with a wet nose and turned away, fiercely rubbing his eyes with a huge paw.
"Damn it, dust got in my eye. Accursed radiation," the werewolf muttered to himself. "Wolves don't cry. Wolves just... sweat with their eyes. Augh."

Yashil looked into the elf's astonished, grateful eyes, and for the first time in his soul blossomed not just hope, but an absolute, indestructible confidence. The world was not hopeless. Hatred was only a thin crust of ice over a frozen river. And he, the little goblin from the Rotten Hollow, had just struck the first breach in it.
